items.creator: UN Resident Coordinator in Bangladesh
items.title: Bangladesh: Situation Report #1 - Flash Flood and Landslides (13 July 2026)
items.author: UN Resident Coordinator in Bangladesh
items.content:
Countries: Bangladesh, Myanmar
Source: UN Resident Coordinator in Bangladesh

Please refer to the attached file.

Bangladesh Flash Floods and Landslides Situation Report #1 (13 July 2026) provides a comprehensive overview of the humanitarian impact of severe monsoon-induced floods and landslides affecting multiple districts across Bangladesh. Heavy rainfall, upstream runoff, and landslide incidents have affected more than 1.11 million people across 10 districts, resulting in 51 reported fatalities, widespread displacement, and significant damage to homes, water systems, roads, bridges, and public infrastructure.The report highlights the evolving emergency situation in Chattogram, Cox’s Bazar, Bandarban, Khagrachhari, Rangamati, Moulvibazar, Sunamganj, Habiganj, Sylhet, and Cumilla, where communities continue to face challenges related to displacement, access constraints, disrupted water and sanitation services, and damage to livelihoods. As of 12 July, more than 38,000 people were staying in over 1,000 evacuation shelters, while humanitarian partners and government authorities were scaling up food assistance, health services, shelter support, cash assistance, and WASH interventions.

items.creator: International Rescue Committee
items.title: PlayMatters Uganda to enhance teacher practices and wellbeing by Learning through Play: A Cost-effectiveness Analysis (CEA), July 2026
items.author: International Rescue Committee
items.content:
Country: Uganda
Source: International Rescue Committee

Please refer to the attached file.

PlayMatters, an education initiative led by IRC in partnership with the LEGO Foundation and a consortium including Plan International, War Child Alliance, Innovations for Poverty Action, and the Behavioural Insights Team, brings Learning through Play (LtP) into refugee-hosting schools across Ethiopia, Uganda, and Tanzania. Since 2020, the program has reached over 14,000 teachers and 900,000 children, replacing rote "chalk-and-talk" teaching with active, child-centered methods designed to improve both learning and wellbeing for refugee and host-community children alike. A new cost-effectiveness analysis, conducted during the program's third cohort in Uganda through a randomized controlled trial, set out to answer a practical question for funders and decision-makers: does this approach deliver enough impact to justify continued investment and scale-up?

The findings suggest it does. Improving a teacher's classroom practice cost roughly $657 per teacher — close to the $668 achieved in an earlier Ethiopia evaluation, where the same model also lifted children's literacy, numeracy, and social-emotional learning. Improving occupational wellbeing cost more, ranging from $2,332 to $6,148 depending on the measure. Compared with the Pakistan Reading Project, a similarly large-scale literacy initiative, PlayMatters achieved comparable or stronger results at similar cost. With low- and middle-income countries facing a $97 billion annual financing gap to meet global education targets, the brief argues that PlayMatters represents a promising, cost-effective model worth replicating and refining across additional contexts.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Terremotos en Venezuela: Reporte de situación #20 (13 de julio de 2026, Hora: 09:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Reporte de situación N.º 20
Terremotos en Venezuela
13 de julio de 2026
Hora: 9:00 pm

Destacados

  • Tras los sismos de magnitud 7,2 y 7,5 del 24 de junio (18:00 hora local), las autoridades reportan más de 1.200 réplicas.
  • Balance oficial: 4.561 personas fallecidas y 16.740 heridas.
  • 6.462 personas han sido rescatadas.
  • Se lanzó un llamamiento adicional por US$298 millones para ampliar la respuesta a los terremotos y atender las necesidades de1,3 millones de personas durante los próximos seis meses.
  • El Sistema de Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en coordinación con el gobierno, responden con acciones multisectoriales y continuarán ampliando la asistencia a las personas afectadas.

Situación general

Venezuela enfrenta una emergencia nacional tras dos sismos consecutivos de gran magnitud (7,2 y 7,5) ocurridos
el 24 de junio (18:00, hora local) en la región centro-norte, con epicentro sobre el eje San Felipe - Yumare - Montalbán (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Las autoridades reportan 1.254 réplicas.

A 19 días de los terremotos, las autoridades reportan un balance de 4.561 personas fallecidas, 16.740 personas heridas y 6.462 personas rescatadas desde el inicio de la emergencia. Asimismo, indicaron que 17.907 personas permanecen sin vivienda.

La respuesta gubernamental mantiene el foco en las labores de búsqueda, la gestión de los campamentos transitorios y la atención de las personas afectadas. Naciones Unidas y sus organizaciones socias, en articulación con las autoridades, realizan acciones multisectoriales para las personas afectadas y continuarán ampliando la respuesta. El Subsecretario General de las Naciones Unidas para Asuntos Humanitarios y Coordinador del Socorro de Emergencia, Tom Fletcher, anunció una adenda al Plan de Respuesta Humanitaria por 298 millones de dólares, destinada a atender las necesidades multisectoriales de 1,3 millones de personas durante los próximos seis meses. Esto se suma a la meta de 5,5 millones de personas que ya contemplaba atenderse con el Plan este 2026, para lo que se requieren 632 millones de dólares.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Earthquakes in Venezuela: Situation Report #20 (13 July 2026, Time: 09:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Situation Report No. 20
Earthquakes in Venezuela
13 July 2026
Time: 9:00 pm

Highlights

  • Following the 24 June earthquakes of magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 (18:00 local time), authorities report more than 1,200 aftershocks.
  • Official toll: 4,561 people dead and 16,740 injured.
  • A total of 6,462 people have been rescued.
  • An additional appeal for US$298 million was launched to scale up the earthquake response and address the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.
  • The United Nations system and its partner organizations, in coordination with the Government, are responding with multisectoral actions and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people.

Situation overview

Venezuela is facing a national emergency following two consecutive high-magnitude earthquakes (7.2 and 7.5) that struck on 24 June (18:00 local time) in the north-central region, with the epicentre located along the San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán axis (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Authorities report 1,254 aftershocks.

Nineteen days since the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,561 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency. Authorities also indicated that 17,907 people remain without housing.

The Government response remains focused on search operations, the management of transitional camps and assistance to affected populations. The United Nations and its partner organizations, in coordination with the authorities, continue implementing multisectoral response activities and scaling up assistance. United Nations Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator Tom Fletcher announced a US$298 million addendum to the Humanitarian Response Plan aimed at addressing the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months. This is in addition to the 5.5 million people already targeted under the 2026 Humanitarian Response Plan, which requires US$632 million in funding.

items.creator: Bangladesh Red Crescent Society
items.title: Bangladesh: BGD: Flood - 07-2026 - Situation Report-8, Monsoon Depression in Cox’s Bazar #8 (2026-07-13)
items.author: Bangladesh Red Crescent Society
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Sources: Bangladesh Red Crescent Society, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

Description

Impact in 33 camps:
Between 5 and 13 July 2026, a total of 518 weather-related incidents were reported, affecting 11,183 households (51,363 people) across the 33 camps. The incident caused damage to 4,656 shelters and displaced 13,038 individuals. To date, 15 fatalities and 22 injuries have been recorded. These figures underscore the significant humanitarian impact of the ongoing monsoon conditions and highlight the need for continued monitoring, preparedness, and response efforts, particularly in areas at heightened risk of flooding, waterlogging, and landslides.

Impact in Host community:
Based on available data, a total of 139,081 affected population across all the sub-districts of Cox’s Bazar. Out of this, 1,195 shelters have been damaged, 124 damaged bridges, with 15 deaths, 19 injured, and 1 missing person reported.

According also to preliminary information, about 2,566 individuals have been evacuated and temporarily accommodated in evacuation centers due to inundation of water from rivers and streams due to the continuous heavy rains in Cox’s Bazar.

At present, BDRCS is closely coordinating with relevant authorities and other humanitarian and development agencies as part of its monsoon monitoring process.

Request For Assistance

Government Requests International Assistance: No

NS Requests International Assistance: Yes

Information Bulletin Published

Completed

Actions taken by Federation

General

Damage/Needs assessment

Search & Rescue

Water & Sanitation

Health

Shelter

Psychosocial support services

Evacuation

First Aid

Relief/Supply distribution

Summary

**Coordination** * BDRCS PMO Emergency Operations Centre remains activated. * BDRCS Camp Focal Points and staff participated in Disaster Management Committee meetings in camps and host communities. * Continued coordination with the RRRC Office, Deputy Commissioner’s Office, and relevant upazila administrations. **Shelter** * A total of **933 verified monsoon-affected households** received emergency shelter assistance in Camps 11 and 12. * Joint Rapid Damage Verification with the Site Management Sector is ongoing to identify and support the remaining affected households. **WASH** * More than **200 community volunteers** were mobilized for WASH facility assessments, hygiene-promotion messaging, and support to affected families in Camps 11, 13, 18, and 19. * Continued coordination with WASH Sector agencies operating in the affected camps. **Livelihoods and Skills Development** * **31 livelihood volunteers and 12 skills trainers** continued disseminating monsoon preparedness and safety messages. * Volunteers and trainers monitored livelihood activities and facilities to identify damage and facilitate timely support. **PGI and CEA** * Collected **398 community feedback reports** related to monsoon damage and needs for referral to relevant sectors through the Community Feedback and Response Mechanism. * Reached **5,832 people** with awareness sessions on child protection, gender-based violence, and community consultation activities in Camps 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, and 19. **Disaster Risk Management** * Provided regular technical guidance to Camp Disaster Management Committees to strengthen emergency response efforts across all **33 camps**. * Mobilized more than **2,000 camp DMU and Site Management volunteers** for early-warning dissemination, emergency search and rescue, and relocation support. * Jointly mobilized more than **1,500 Cyclone Preparedness Programme volunteers** for awareness messaging and emergency search-and-rescue activities across Cox’s Bazar upazilas. * Continued coordination with Site Management, relevant sectors, the Bangladesh Meteorological Department, CPP, and the RCP-EPR Working Group to identify and address response needs and gaps. **Health** * Reached **7,331 individuals** through awareness sessions, household visits, and mental health and psychosocial support activities. * Provided health information, referral assistance, and follow-up support to vulnerable community members. * Continued essential clinical services, including outpatient and maternity care. * Maintained immediate-response capacity to restore the functionality of health facilities following any reported damage or incident. **BDRCS Unit Office** * Distributed dry-food assistance to **700 monsoon-affected households** in Pekua and Kutubdia upazilas.

Actions taken by RCRC

General

Search & Rescue

Water & Sanitation

Health

Shelter

Psychosocial support services

Evacuation

First Aid

Relief/Supply distribution

Human remains management and identification

Summary

**Coordination** * BDRCS PMO Emergency Operations Centre remains activated. * BDRCS Camp Focal Points and staff participated in Disaster Management Committee meetings in camps and host communities. * Continued coordination with the RRRC Office, Deputy Commissioner’s Office, and relevant upazila administrations. **Shelter** * A total of **933 verified monsoon-affected households** received WASH Sector agencies operating in the affected camps. **Livelihoods and Skills Development** * **31 livelihood volunteers and 12 skills trainers** continued disseminating monsoon preparedness and safety messages. * Volunteers and trainers monitored livelihood activities and facilities to identify damage and facilitate timely support. **PGI and CEA** * Collected **398 community feedback reports** related to monsoon damage and needs for referral to relevant sectors through the Community Feedback and Response Mechanism. * Reached **5,832 people** with awareness sessions on child protection, gender-based violence, and community consultation activities in Camps 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, and 19. **Disaster Risk Management** * Provided regular technical guidance to Camp Disaster Management Committees to strengthen emergency response efforts across all **33 camps**. * Mobilized more than **2,000 camp DMU and Site Management volunteers** for early-warning dissemination, emergency search and rescue, and relocation support. * Jointly mobilized more than **1,500 Cyclone Preparedness Programme volunteers** for awareness messaging and emergency search-and-rescue activities across Cox’s Bazar upazilas. * Continued coordination with Site Management, relevant sectors, the Bangladesh Meteorological Department, CPP, and the RCP-EPR Working Group to identify and address response needs and gaps. **Health** * Reached **7,331 individuals** through awareness sessions, household visits, and mental health and psychosocial support activities. * Provided health information, referral assistance, and follow-up support to vulnerable community members. * Continued essential clinical services, including outpatient and maternity care. * Maintained immediate-response capacity to restore the functionality of health facilities following any reported damage or incident. **BDRCS Unit Office** * Distributed dry-food assistance to **700 monsoon-affected households** in Pekua and Kutubdia upazilas.

Actions taken by National Society

General

Damage/Needs assessment

Water & Sanitation

Food aid

Health

Shelter

Psychosocial support services

Evacuation

First Aid

Relief/Supply distribution

Human remains management and identification

Summary

**Coordination** * BDRCS PMO Emergency Operations Centre remains activated. * BDRCS Camp Focal Points and staff participated in Disaster Management Committee meetings in camps and host communities. * Continued coordination with the RRRC Office, Deputy Commissioner’s Office, and relevant upazila administrations. **Shelter** * A total of **933 verified monsoon-affected households** received emergency shelter assistance in Camps 11 and 12. * Joint Rapid Damage Verification with the Site Management Sector is ongoing to identify and support the remaining affected households. **WASH** * More than **200 community volunteers** were mobilized for WASH facility assessments, hygiene-promotion messaging, and support to affected families in Camps 11, 13, 18, and 19. * Continued coordination with WASH Sector agencies operating in the affected camps. **Livelihoods and Skills Development** * **31 livelihood volunteers and 12 skills trainers** continued disseminating monsoon preparedness and safety messages. * Volunteers and trainers monitored livelihood activities and facilities to identify damage and facilitate timely support. **PGI and CEA** * Collected **398 community feedback reports** related to monsoon damage and needs for referral to relevant sectors through the Community Feedback and Response Mechanism. * Reached **5,832 people** with awareness sessions on child protection, gender-based violence, and community consultation activities in Camps 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, and 19. **Disaster Risk Management** * Provided regular technical guidance to Camp Disaster Management Committees to strengthen emergency response efforts across all **33 camps**. * Mobilized more than **2,000 camp DMU and Site Management volunteers** for early-warning dissemination, emergency search and rescue, and relocation support. * Jointly mobilized more than **1,500 Cyclone Preparedness Programme volunteers** for awareness messaging and emergency search-and-rescue activities across Cox’s Bazar upazilas. * Continued coordination with Site Management, relevant sectors, the Bangladesh Meteorological Department, CPP, and the RCP-EPR Working Group to identify and address response needs and gaps. **Health** * Reached **7,331 individuals** through awareness sessions, household visits, and mental health and psychosocial support activities. * Provided health information, referral assistance, and follow-up support to vulnerable community members. * Continued essential clinical services, including outpatient and maternity care. * Maintained immediate-response capacity to restore the functionality of health facilities following any reported damage or incident. **BDRCS Unit Office** * Distributed dry-food assistance to **700 monsoon-affected households** in Pekua and Kutubdia upazilas.

Actions taken by others

In the camp settlement, the Government of Bangladesh, through the Office of the Refugee Relief and Repatriation Commissioner (RRRC), is monitoring the overall situation through camp-level Disaster Management Committees (DMCs) to support timely response and a standardized assistance approach. Fire Service and Civil Defense (FSCD), together with Armed Police Battalion (APBn), is engaged in emergency search and rescue operations, while the Bangladesh Armed Forces Division (AFD) remains on standby to provide support as needed. In host communities, the Deputy Commissioner's Office has activated the district-level EOC to monitor early actions by the Cyclone Preparedness Programme (CPP), BDRCS local branch and sub-district administrations. The Deputy Commissioner's Office has announced humanitarian assistance for affected populations, although the support remains inadequate compared with needs. More than 700 emergency shelters have also been opened to accommodate evacuees from at-risk areas and reduce loss of life from landslides and flooding. In addition, Government authorities including RRRC Office and Deputy Commissioner (DC) Office-Cox's Bazar have both kept provision of continuous monitoring of the situation through their EOC. In addition, there is provision of triggering contingency funds allocated for districts which will be very much specific to the host community only. For camp settlement, from the Government end they have their provision to monitor the situation and work closely with humanitarian partners in responding effectively and efficiently based on further escalation of the monsoon crisis in the near future. Led by IOM and UNHCR and implemented through a network of national and international NGOs including ACTED, BRAC, DRC, MBSS, NRC, CARE, Oxfam, BDRCS, IRC, World Vision, Mukti and others, the emergency response has focused on life-saving shelter, site management, WASH, protection, health, nutrition, food security and education interventions. Partners are conducting rapid damage assessments, relocating at-risk households, supporting rescue operations, providing temporary shelter and emergency food assistance, repairing critical WASH facilities, delivering psychosocial support and protection referrals, disseminating early warning messages, and maintaining essential services despite challenging access conditions. The Rohingya Coordination Platform, jointly with the RRRC Office, has also issued joint messaging to accelerate monsoon preparedness and scale up community readiness across all 33 camps. On the other hand, UNDP and other key partners are actively engaged in host community programming have triggered contingency funds (an amount of 300,000 BDT for Ukhiya and Teknaf sub-districts) to support sub-district administrations in providing humanitarian assistance to affected families.

Planned International Response

DREF: Requested

emergency shelter assistance in Camps 11 and 12. * Joint Rapid Damage Verification with the Site Management Sector is ongoing to identify and support the remaining affected households. **WASH** * More than **200 community volunteers** were mobilized for WASH facility assessments, hygiene-promotion messaging, and support to affected families in Camps 11, 13, 18, and 19. * Continued coordination with WASH Sector agencies operating in the affected camps. **Livelihoods and Skills Development** * **31 livelihood volunteers and 12 skills trainers** continued disseminating monsoon preparedness and safety messages. * Volunteers and trainers monitored livelihood activities and facilities to identify damage and facilitate timely support. **PGI and CEA** * Collected **398 community feedback reports** related to monsoon damage and needs for referral to relevant sectors through the Community Feedback and Response Mechanism. * Reached **5,832 people** with awareness sessions on child protection, gender-based violence, and community consultation activities in Camps 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, and 19. **Disaster Risk Management** * Provided regular technical guidance to Camp Disaster Management Committees to strengthen emergency response efforts across all **33 camps**. * Mobilized more than **2,000 camp DMU and Site Management volunteers** for early-warning dissemination, emergency search and rescue, and relocation support. * Jointly mobilized more than **1,500 Cyclone Preparedness Programme volunteers** for awareness messaging and emergency search-and-rescue activities across Cox’s Bazar upazilas. * Continued coordination with Site Management, relevant sectors, the Bangladesh Meteorological Department, CPP, and the RCP-EPR Working Group to identify and address response needs and gaps. **Health** * Reached **7,331 individuals** through awareness sessions, household visits, and mental health and psychosocial support activities. * Provided health information, referral assistance, and follow-up support to vulnerable community members. * Continued essential clinical services, including outpatient and maternity care. * Maintained immediate-response capacity to restore the functionality of health facilities following any reported damage or incident. **BDRCS Unit Office** * Distributed dry-food assistance to **700 monsoon-affected households** in Pekua and Kutubdia upazilas.
Actions taken by National Society
General
Damage/Needs assessment
Water & Sanitation
Food aid
Health
Shelter
Psychosocial support services
Evacuation
First Aid
Relief/Supply distribution
Human remains management and identification
Summary
**Coordination** * BDRCS PMO Emergency Operations Centre remains activated. * BDRCS Camp Focal Points and staff participated in Disaster Management Committee meetings in camps and host communities. * Continued coordination with the RRRC Office, Deputy Commissioner’s Office, and relevant upazila administrations. **Shelter** * A total of **933 verified monsoon-affected households** received emergency shelter assistance in Camps 11 and 12. * Joint Rapid Damage Verification with the Site Management Sector is ongoing to identify and support the remaining affected households. **WASH** * More than **200 community volunteers** were mobilized for WASH facility assessments, hygiene-promotion messaging, and support to affected families in Camps 11, 13, 18, and 19. * Continued coordination with WASH Sector agencies operating in the affected camps. **Livelihoods and Skills Development** * **31 livelihood volunteers and 12 skills trainers** continued disseminating monsoon preparedness and safety messages. * Volunteers and trainers monitored livelihood activities and facilities to identify damage and facilitate timely support. **PGI and CEA** * Collected **398 community feedback reports** related to monsoon damage and needs for referral to relevant sectors through the Community Feedback and Response Mechanism. * Reached **5,832 people** with awareness sessions on child protection, gender-based violence, and community consultation activities in Camps 11, 12, 13, 15, 18, and 19. **Disaster Risk Management** * Provided regular technical guidance to Camp Disaster Management Committees to strengthen emergency response efforts across all **33 camps**. * Mobilized more than **2,000 camp DMU and Site Management volunteers** for early-warning dissemination, emergency search and rescue, and relocation support. * Jointly mobilized more than **1,500 Cyclone Preparedness Programme volunteers** for awareness messaging and emergency search-and-rescue activities across Cox’s Bazar upazilas. * Continued coordination with Site Management, relevant sectors, the Bangladesh Meteorological Department, CPP, and the RCP-EPR Working Group to identify and address response needs and gaps. **Health** * Reached **7,331 individuals** through awareness sessions, household visits, and mental health and psychosocial support activities. * Provided health information, referral assistance, and follow-up support to vulnerable community members. * Continued essential clinical services, including outpatient and maternity care. * Maintained immediate-response capacity to restore the functionality of health facilities following any reported damage or incident. **BDRCS Unit Office** * Distributed dry-food assistance to **700 monsoon-affected households** in Pekua and Kutubdia upazilas.
Actions taken by others
In the camp settlement, the Government of Bangladesh, through the Office of the Refugee Relief and Repatriation Commissioner (RRRC), is monitoring the overall situation through camp-level Disaster Management Committees (DMCs) to support timely response and a standardized assistance approach. Fire Service and Civil Defense (FSCD), together with Armed Police Battalion (APBn), is engaged in emergency search and rescue operations, while the Bangladesh Armed Forces Division (AFD) remains on standby to provide support as needed. In host communities, the Deputy Commissioner's Office has activated the district-level EOC to monitor early actions by the Cyclone Preparedness Programme (CPP), BDRCS local branch and sub-district administrations. The Deputy Commissioner's Office has announced humanitarian assistance for affected populations, although the support remains inadequate compared with needs. More than 700 emergency shelters have also been opened to accommodate evacuees from at-risk areas and reduce loss of life from landslides and flooding. In addition, Government authorities including RRRC Office and Deputy Commissioner (DC) Office-Cox's Bazar have both kept provision of continuous monitoring of the situation through their EOC. In addition, there is provision of triggering contingency funds allocated for districts which will be very much specific to the host community only. For camp settlement, from the Government end they have their provision to monitor the situation and work closely with humanitarian partners in responding effectively and efficiently based on further escalation of the monsoon crisis in the near future. Led by IOM and UNHCR and implemented through a network of national and international NGOs including ACTED, BRAC, DRC, MBSS, NRC, CARE, Oxfam, BDRCS, IRC, World Vision, Mukti and others, the emergency response has focused on life-saving shelter, site management, WASH, protection, health, nutrition, food security and education interventions. Partners are conducting rapid damage assessments, relocating at-risk households, supporting rescue operations, providing temporary shelter and emergency food assistance, repairing critical WASH facilities, delivering psychosocial support and protection referrals, disseminating early warning messages, and maintaining essential services despite challenging access conditions. The Rohingya Coordination Platform, jointly with the RRRC Office, has also issued joint messaging to accelerate monsoon preparedness and scale up community readiness across all 33 camps. On the other hand, UNDP and other key partners are actively engaged in host community programming have triggered contingency funds (an amount of 300,000 BDT for Ukhiya and Teknaf sub-districts) to support sub-district administrations in providing humanitarian assistance to affected families.
Planned International Response
DREF: Requested
categories: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

items.creator: Emergency Telecommunications Cluster
items.title: DR Congo: DRC: Ebola BVD Outbreak: LTC Situation Report (Telecoms) #6 (Reporting period: 06 - 12 July 2026)
items.author: Emergency Telecommunications Cluster
items.content:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Sources: Emergency Telecommunications Cluster, Logistics Cluster, World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

This situation report provides updates on the Ebola Bundibugyo Virus Disease (BVD) Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). It outlines planned and ongoing telecommunications activities of the Logistics & Telecommunications Cluster1 (LTC) Telecoms to support the ongoing humanitarian response.

Summary Points

• The Ebola Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in the DRC continues to expand, with more than 1,700 confirmed cases reported as of 09 July. As pressure on surveillance, coordination, and information management increases, reliable telecommunications services remain critical to support an effective response.

• LTC Telecoms and the United Nations Department of Safety and Security (UNDSS) restored the dedicated UN security communications channel in Bunia after six months of downtime and have begun establishing a dedicated channel for NGO partners, strengthening security communications for responders operating across Ituri.

• LTC Telecoms launched a partner connectivity mapping exercise to identify existing and planned satellite connectivity deployments supporting the Ebola response. The initiative will help identify coverage gaps, avoid duplication, and guide the prioritization of telecommunications support in affected areas. While the survey remains openended, partners are encouraged to complete it as soon as possible to support timely analysis and coordination.

items.creator: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.title: Zimbabwe Population Movement 2026 - DREF Operation (MDRZW028), 13 July 2026
items.author: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.content:
Countries: Zimbabwe, South Africa
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

Description of the Event
Date when the trigger was met
25-06-2026

What happened, where and when?

The ongoing situation involves the large-scale return of Zimbabwean nationals from South Africa following a sharp escalation of localised violence targeting foreign nationals between March and June 2026. The violence, concentrated in major urban centres including Johannesburg, Durban, Cape Town, and parts of KwaZulu-Natal and the Eastern Cape (including Port Elizabeth), resulted in intimidation, displacement, loss of access to basic services, and heightened insecurity among migrant communities, prompting large-scale voluntary and assisted return movements to Zimbabwe. According to the Department of Immigration, a total of over 40,198 Zimbabwean returnees has self-repatriated since 28 May 2026, including 4,795 migrants who have been assisted through the State-assisted repatriation program (as of 27 June 2026).

The primary entry point and reception area is Beitbridge Border Post in Matabeleland South Province, Zimbabwe, where returnees are processed at the Beitbridge Reception Centre before onward movement to their areas of origin across the country, including Harare, Bulawayo, Manicaland, Masvingo, Midlands, Mashonaland East, Mashonaland West and Mashonaland Central Provinces.

The violence escalated between March and June 2026, with return movements beginning in late May 2026 and continuing to date. The Government of Zimbabwe has convened a series of coordination meetings at national and district levels to manage and coordinate assistance for returning migrants, including an interministerial meeting held on 27 May 2026, during which the Department of Immigration reported the consolidated statistics on return movements. Although returns have been ongoing over several months, the current phase of sustained high-volume arrivals, combined with winter conditions, makes this the appropriate time for DREF activation, as humanitarian needs have intensified due to prolonged waiting times, overnight stays at the reception centre, and exposure to low temperatures.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Monthly Monitoring of Forced Returns to Haiti (June 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Countries: Haiti, Bahamas, Dominican Republic, Jamaica, Turks and Caicos Islands, United States of America
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Under the Migrant Protection Working Group, the National Office for Migration (ONM), the International Organization for Migration (IOM), and the Support Group for Repatriates and Refugees (GARR) gather data on the volume of forced return flows at Haiti’s entry points. This information is used to guide appropriate assistance mechanisms for individuals who have been forcibly returned. The collected data includes the number of forcibly returned individuals (through systematic counting), the dates of their returns, and the countries from which they departed.

This report presents an overview of forced return flows observed in June 2026, during which 26,568 people were forcibly returned to Haiti, a 3 per cent increase compared to May 2026, making June the month with the highest number of forced returns since the start of 2026. Compared to June 2025, the number of forced returns during this month rose by 14 per cent. Furthermore, over the first six months of 2026, the number of forcibly returned individuals amounts to 141,720, an 11 per cent increase compared to the same period in 2025, signaling an upward trend in forced returns in 2026 compared to previous years. The border crossings of Ouanaminthe and Malpasse saw the most significant increases between the first halves of 2025 and 2026, with rises of 58 and 165 percent, respectively.

Explore the complete dataset collected since 2021 via the interactive dashboard.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Haiti, Bahamas, Dominican Republic, Jamaica, Turks and Caicos Islands, United States of America
Source: International Organization for Migration

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Haiti: Suivi Mensuel des Retours Forcés en Haïti (Juin 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Countries: Haiti, Bahamas, Dominican Republic, Jamaica, Turks and Caicos Islands, United States of America
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Dans le cadre du Groupe de travail sur la protection des migrants, l'Office National de la Migration (ONM), l'Organisation Internationale pour les Migrations (OIM) et le Groupe d’Appui aux Rapatriés et Réfugiés (GARR) collectent des données sur le volume des flux de retour forcé aux points d'entrée en Haïti. Ces informations sont utilisées pour orienter les mécanismes d'assistance appropriés aux personnes retournées de force. Les données collectées comprennent le nombre de personnes retournées de force (collecté par comptage systématique), les dates de leur retour et les pays de départ.

Ce rapport présente un aperçu des flux de retours forcés observés en juin 2026, au cours duquel 26 568 personnes ont été retournées de force en Haïti, soit une hausse de 3 pour cent par rapport à mai 2026, faisant du mois de juin celui ayant enregistré le plus grand nombre de retours forcés depuis le début de l’année 2026. En comparaison à juin 2025, le nombre de retours forcés durant ce mois a connu une augmentation de 14 pour cent. Par ailleurs, sur les six premiers mois de l’année 2026, le nombre de personnes retournées de force s’élève à 141 720 personnes, une hausse de 11 pour cent par rapport à la même période en 2025, ce qui annonce une tendance à la hausse des retours forcés en 2026 comparativement aux années précédentes. Les points frontaliers de Ouanaminthe et Malpasse ont connu les augmentations les plus importantes entre les premiers semestres 2025 et 2026, avec respectivement 58 et 165 pour cent de hausse.

Explorez l'ensemble des données collectées depuis 2021 via le tableau de bord interactif.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Solomon Islands: Accountability to Affected People Analysis Report | Tropical Cyclone Maila
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: Solomon Islands
Sources: International Organization for Migration, Pacific Humanitarian Team, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, United Nations Population Fund

Please refer to the attached file.

The July 2026 Accountability to Affected People (AAP) report by the Pacific Humanitarian Team analyzes community and organizational perspectives following Category 5 Tropical Cyclone Maila, which affected 150,000 people across the Western and Choiseul provinces in the Solomon Islands. By comparing survey data from 14 humanitarian organizations with community-level assessments from 595 displaced households in Western Province, the evaluation examines the effectiveness of information provision, community engagement, and sensitive feedback mechanisms. The findings indicate that while organizations have foundational two-way communication and complaint systems in place, significant gaps persist regarding community awareness of these systems, consistent follow-through on complaints, and the inclusion of marginalized populations such as persons with disabilities and individuals with diverse SOGIESC. To maintain community trust and mitigate serious protection risks like sexual exploitation and corruption, the report recommends diversifying communication toward trusted media such as radio, standardizing feedback loops, and prioritizing AAP and protection refresher trainings for humanitarian staff.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Solomon Islands
Sources: International Organization for Migration, Pacific Humanitarian Team, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, United Nations Population Fund

Please refer to the attached file.

By comparing survey data from 14 humanitarian organizations with community-level assessments from 595 displaced households in Western Province, the evaluation examines the effectiveness of information provision, community engagement, and sensitive feedback mechanisms. The findings indicate that while organizations have foundational two-way communication and complaint systems in place, significant gaps persist regarding community awareness of these systems, consistent follow-through on complaints, and the inclusion of marginalized populations such as persons with disabilities and individuals with diverse SOGIESC. To maintain community trust and mitigate serious protection risks like sexual exploitation and corruption, the report recommends diversifying communication toward trusted media such as radio, standardizing feedback loops, and prioritizing AAP and protection refresher trainings for humanitarian staff.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Occupied Palestinian Territory Emergency Response: Activity Update #57, 1 - 30 June 2026 [EN/AR]
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached files.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

Critical supply and funding shortages persisted through June, deepening the humanitarian needs and compounding the suffering of Palestinians largely confined to a shrinking portion of Gaza. Kerem Shalom/ Karam Abu Salam remained the only operational crossing for cargo following the closure of Zikim crossing on 24 May. The access-restricted "Orange Line" was extended twice during June, reaching 64.9 per cent of the Gaza Strip by 23 June, with the Israeli leadership reportedly ordering a further expansion to cover up to 70 per cent of the land.

Most of Gaza's population remained displaced across overcrowded sites, two thirds of which have no Site Management Cluster partner presence. Rising summer temperatures and persistent use of open flames for cooking inside highly flammable tents saw an increase in domestic fire incidents. Addressing pest and rodent infestations remained a top priority response. However, these efforts were constrained by the lack of space in crowded sites and insufficient equipment needed to manage accumulated waste and debris, which provide breeding grounds for rodents and pests to multiply.

Shelter needs remained acute, with 70 per cent of the population lacking adequate shelter. Newly displaced families frequently fled without their tents or belongings, and prolonged restrictions on the entry of critical shelter materials continued to constrain the response.

Briefing the Security Council on 18 June, UN Relief Chief Tom Fletcher warned that Gaza was "being held together by humanitarian workarounds and Palestinian perseverance," which was unsustainable, and that civilians could not wait for diplomacy to receive the basics for survival. He called for the immediate, full-capacity operation of multiple crossings, the removal of restrictions on essential survival items, and funding that is timely, flexible and commensurate with the scale of the crisis.

Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: International Organization for Migration

SITUATION OVERVIEW
Critical supply and funding shortages persisted through June, deepening the humanitarian needs and compounding the suffering of Palestinians largely confined to a shrinking portion of Gaza. Kerem Shalom/ Karam Abu Salam remained the only operational crossing for cargo following the closure of Zikim crossing on 24 May. The access-restricted "Orange Line" was extended twice during June, reaching 64.9 per cent of the Gaza Strip by 23 June, with the Israeli leadership reportedly ordering a further expansion to cover up to 70 per cent of the land.
Most of Gaza's population remained displaced across overcrowded sites, two thirds of which have no Site Management Cluster partner presence. Rising summer temperatures and persistent use of open flames for cooking inside highly flammable tents saw an increase in domestic fire incidents. Addressing pest and rodent infestations remained a top priority response. However, these efforts were constrained by the lack of space in crowded sites and insufficient equipment needed to manage accumulated waste and debris, which provide breeding grounds for rodents and pests to multiply.
Shelter needs remained acute, with 70 per cent of the population lacking adequate shelter. Newly displaced families frequently fled without their tents or belongings, and prolonged restrictions on the entry of critical shelter materials continued to constrain the response.
Briefing the Security Council on 18 June, UN Relief Chief Tom Fletcher warned that Gaza was "being held together by humanitarian workarounds and Palestinian perseverance," which was unsustainable, and that civilians could not wait for diplomacy to receive the basics for survival. He called for the immediate, full-capacity operation of multiple crossings, the removal of restrictions on essential survival items, and funding that is timely, flexible and commensurate with the scale of the crisis.

items.creator: World Health Organization
items.title: Knowledge Management Series for Health 2026: Analytical Factsheet - Skilled Birth Attendance and Newborn Survival in South Sudan (June 2026)
items.author: World Health Organization
items.content:
Country: South Sudan
Source: World Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

The Skilled Birth Attendance (SBA) serves as the main framework for evaluating newborn health. By analyzing national and sub-national trends, geographic disparities, system readiness, and survival outcomes through SBA, we gain a comprehensive understanding of both advancements and ongoing challenges. This analytical factsheet synthesizes findings from MICS 2025 (population level data), DHIS2 routine data (facility level data), national reports, and other sources to uncover systematic gaps leading to high nevborn deaths. It also pinpoints areas where interventions are most likely to save newborn lives.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: South Sudan
Source: World Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

items.creator: UN Children's Fund
items.title: UNICEF Venezuela Humanitarian Situation Report No.4 (Earthquake Response), 02 - 12 July 2026
items.author: UN Children's Fund
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Children's Fund

Please refer to the attached file.

Highlights

  • Two weeks after two powerful earthquakes struck north-central Venezuela on 24 June 2026, official figures report more than 4,490 deaths, 16,740 people injured and 17,907 people left without housing. More than 1,200 aftershocks have been recorded, while damage assessments continue.
  • UNICEF is delivering life-saving and protective services in the initial phase of the response. This includes WASH services across three temporary camps in La Guaira reaching more than 3,500 people. Hygiene supplies have been distributed to more than 6,900 people across temporary camps. Mobile health teams have been deployed across affected areas providing medical services to over 3,100 people. Child-Friendly Spaces are operational in four temporary camps in La Guaira, providing regular psychosocial support and recreational activities to more than 890 children and adolescents.
  • UNICEF continues to scale up its humanitarian response, having distributed at least 82 metric tons of emergency supplies. The supplies include emergency health kits, water purification and storage equipment, tents for child-friendly spaces and service delivery points, and early childhood development and recreational kits.
  • In addition to the US$137.6 million requested under the 2026 Humanitarian Action for Children (HAC) appeal and aligned with the addendum to the Humanitarian Response Plan 2026, UNICEF requires US$65.7 million to respond to the humanitarian impact of the earthquakes.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Children's Fund

Please refer to the attached file.
Highlights
Two weeks after two powerful earthquakes struck north-central Venezuela on 24 June 2026, official figures report more than 4,490 deaths, 16,740 people injured and 17,907 people left without housing. More than 1,200 aftershocks have been recorded, while damage assessments continue.
UNICEF is delivering life-saving and protective services in the initial phase of the response. This includes WASH services across three temporary camps in La Guaira reaching more than 3,500 people. Hygiene supplies have been distributed to more than 6,900 people across temporary camps. Mobile health teams have been deployed across affected areas providing medical services to over 3,100 people. Child-Friendly Spaces are operational in four temporary camps in La Guaira, providing regular psychosocial support and recreational activities to more than 890 children and adolescents.
UNICEF continues to scale up its humanitarian response, having distributed at least 82 metric tons of emergency supplies. The supplies include emergency health kits, water purification and storage equipment, tents for child-friendly spaces and service delivery points, and early childhood development and recreational kits.
In addition to the US$137.6 million requested under the 2026 Humanitarian Action for Children (HAC) appeal and aligned with the addendum to the Humanitarian Response Plan 2026, UNICEF requires US$65.7 million to respond to the humanitarian impact of the earthquakes.

items.creator: UN Children's Fund
items.title: South Sudan Humanitarian Flash Update (Level 2 Emergency Response & BVD Preparedness), 01 May - 30 June 2026
items.author: UN Children's Fund
items.content:
Countries: South Sudan, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda
Source: UN Children's Fund

Please refer to the attached file.

Situation Overview:

The humanitarian situation in Jonglei State and parts of Upper Nile, Unity and Lakes States remains fragile, fluid, and highly complex, following months of intense conflict that began in late 2025. While relative calm has been observed in some locations, enabling the gradual scale-up of humanitarian assistance, conditions remain volatile, with localized insecurity, intercommunal tensions, and proximity to active frontlines continuing to pose significant risks. Between January and May, nearly 430,000 people were newly displaced due to violence across South Sudan, including over 320,000 in Jonglei State. An estimated 260,000 people remain displaced within Jonglei, while others have sought refuge in neighboring areas, including Mingkaman (Lakes State), Upper Nile, and Juba County (Central Equatoria). Population movements remain dynamic, with some returns reported in areas such as Akobo, alongside continued displacement in other locations.

Despite some stabilization in parts of Jonglei, humanitarian needs remain severe. The return of previously displaced populations to areas with limited services, coupled with continued population inflows, is placing significant pressure on already overstretched systems. The health system remains under acute strain, with Akobo County Hospital serving an estimated 267,000 people, nearly five times its intended capacity, while Akobo West has minimal functional health infrastructure, leaving many communities with limited access to essential services. Access to education remains disrupted, largely due to shortages of teachers and damaged or non-functional learning spaces. Water supply systems continue to deteriorate amid unreliable power supply, reducing access to safe drinking water and heightening public health risks, including waterborne diseases. Protection risks remain high, with continued reports of child separation, conflict-related sexual violence, and recruitment of children by armed groups. These risks are exacerbated by ongoing population movements and intercommunal tensions, particularly in areas of return and displacement.

The situation is further compounded by severe food insecurity and worsening malnutrition. Between April and July 2026, an estimated 7.8 million people across South Sudan are facing high levels of acute food insecurity (IPC Phase 3 or above), including approximately 73,000 people in Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) in counties such as Akobo, Fangak, Nyirol, and Uror in Jonglei, and Ulang and Nasir in Upper Nile. In Jonglei alone, around 1.4 million people (64 per cent of the population) are projected to face Crisis or worse levels of food insecurity. The nutrition situation in Jonglei is similarly critical, with an estimated 500,000 children aged 6–59 months requiring treatment for acute malnutrition, including over 172,000 cases of severe acute malnutrition, reflecting an extremely high disease and mortality risk burden. Several counties including Akobo, Duk, Fangak, and Uror, are classified in IPC Acute Malnutrition Phase 5 (Extremely Critical), indicating widespread and life threatening levels of malnutrition.

In late March, UNICEF activated a Level 2 (L2) emergency response to scale up life-saving assistance in the most affected areas as the humanitarian situation deteriorated due to escalating conflict, displacement, and public health risks. The activation strengthened surge capacity, enhanced field presence, and accelerated decision-making to enable a more timely and coordinated response. It reinforced integrated, multi-sectoral service delivery across health, nutrition, WASH, education, and protection, particularly in high-risk and hardto-reach areas. It also improved supply chain efficiency, strengthened coordination with partners, and reinforced accountability to affected populations, including sustained Prevention of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA) efforts across all interventions.
